figures.caption: Figure 4: Histogram over samples x1 and x2 values for simulated and experimental results for the inverse problem in (26) with T = 2, p = 4 and ε = 0.05.

Table 3: Euclidean distance ∥xrec−xtrue∥ and per-coefficient absolute errors |xrec,ℓ−xtrue,ℓ| for the bestby-cost and mean estimates (T = 4, p = 3, ε = 0.03, Ns = 10,000 shots). Since the Haar basis matrix Φ has orthonormal columns, the signal reconstruction error satisfies ∥f rec − f true∥ = ∥xrec − xtrue∥ and is therefore equal to the final column.
Figure 7: Cost distributions for varying bits per variable for solving the inverse problem in (29) with 5 wavelet coefficients on the VTT Q50 Quantum computer with T = 4, p = 3 and ε = 0.03.

Figure 1: Simulations for the one-dimensional inverse problem in (25) when comparing parameter values time T and time discretization steps p. As T and p increase, the solution concentrates more sharply around the true value xtrue = 1.05962.

Figure 2: Results for the one-dimensional inverse problem in (25) on the VTT Q50 quantum processor. Making the circuit deeper accumulates quantum errors, motivating the choice of smaller T and p values for further experiments.
Figure 8: Recovered wavelet coefficients for varying bits per variable for solving the inverse problem in (29) on the VTT Q50 Quantum computer with T = 4, p = 3 and ε = 0.03.
Figure 9: Recovered signal for varying bits per variable for solving the inverse problem in (29) with 5 wavelet coefficients on the VTT Q50 Quantum computer with T = 4, p = 3 and ε = 0.03.
Table 2: Circuit and result statistics for the Haar wavelet experiment (T = 4, p = 3, ε = 0.03, Ns = 10,000 shots) on the VTT Q50.

Figure 5: Theoretical posterior geometry and empirical sample distributions for the inverse problem (26) with T = 2, p = 4, and ε = 0.05. The left panel shows the eigendirections and credible regions of the continuous posterior, while the center and right panels show the corresponding sample distributions obtained from simulation and the VTT Q50 quantum processor, respectively.

Figure 6: Spectral gap γ(s) = E1(s)−E0(s) of the interpolating Hamiltonian H(s) = (1− s)HB + sHC for problem (26) (n = 12 qubits, ε = 0.05). Left: ground and first excited state energies E0(s) and E1(s). Right: spectral gap, which decreases monotonically from γ(0) = 2 to ∆min = 0.001020 at s = 1, implying an adiabatic time requirement of order 107, far exceeding the chosen T = 2.
